questions.prompt: Which access order defines a stack?

answers.text: Push and pop normally operate at the same end.

feedback.legit: Correct. Push and pop normally operate at the same end.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Push and pop normally operate at the same end.

answers.text: Last in, first out.

feedback.legit: Correct. Last in, first out.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Last in, first out.

answers.text: First in, first out describes a queue.

feedback.legit: Correct. First in, first out describes a queue.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. First in, first out describes a queue.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: Which access order defines a basic queue?

answers.text: First in, first out.

feedback.legit: Correct. First in, first out.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. First in, first out.

answers.text: The newest item always leaves first.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first.
feedback.sus: Correct. A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first.

answers.text: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.

feedback.legit: Correct. Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.

questions.prompt: What prerequisite does ordinary binary search need?

answers.text: The target must be present.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent.
feedback.sus: Correct. Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent.

answers.text: Binary search always runs in O(log n) time on a linked list.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply.
feedback.sus: Correct. Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply.

answers.text: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.

feedback.legit: Correct. The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

answers.text: Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost.

feedback.legit: Correct. Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost.

answers.text: Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.

feedback.legit: Correct. Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.

answers.text: Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor.

feedback.legit: Correct. Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor.

questions.prompt: When does breadth-first search find shortest paths?

answers.text: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.

feedback.legit: Correct. It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.

answers.text: It directly handles arbitrary negative edge weights.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights.
feedback.sus: Correct. Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights.

answers.text: It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.

feedback.legit: Correct. It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.

questions.prompt: What does stability mean for a sorting algorithm?

answers.text: The algorithm never uses extra memory.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Stability and memory usage are separate properties.
feedback.sus: Correct. Stability and memory usage are separate properties.

answers.text: Every implementation of quicksort is stable.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation.
feedback.sus: Correct. Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation.

answers.text: Equal-key items keep their original relative order.

feedback.legit: Correct. Equal-key items keep their original relative order.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Equal-key items keep their original relative order.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: What does a binary min-heap guarantee?

answers.text: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.

feedback.legit: Correct. Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.

answers.text: The minimum element is at the root.

feedback.legit: Correct. The minimum element is at the root.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. The minimum element is at the root.

answers.text: Every level is globally sorted from left to right.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property.
feedback.sus: Correct. A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: What problem does a disjoint-set union structure solve?

answers.text: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.

feedback.legit: Correct. It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.

answers.text: It returns the shortest weighted path between two vertices.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length.
feedback.sus: Correct. Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length.

answers.text: Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.

feedback.legit: Correct. Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.

questions.prompt: When is dynamic programming a useful approach?

answers.text: Whenever recursion appears, regardless of repeated work.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful.
feedback.sus: Correct. Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful.

answers.text: Dynamic programming always chooses the locally best option at each step.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general.
feedback.sus: Correct. Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general.

answers.text: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.

feedback.legit: Correct. When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.

questions.prompt: Can standard Dijkstra's algorithm handle negative edge weights?

answers.text: Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights.

feedback.legit: Correct. Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights.

answers.text: Yes, as long as there is no negative cycle.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step.
feedback.sus: Correct. A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step.

answers.text: Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.

feedback.legit: Correct. Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.

questions.prompt: When does a topological ordering exist?

answers.text: For a directed acyclic graph.

feedback.legit: Correct. For a directed acyclic graph.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. For a directed acyclic graph.

answers.text: For every directed graph.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A directed cycle makes a topological ordering impossible.
feedback.sus: Correct. A directed cycle makes a topological ordering impossible.

answers.text: A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering.

feedback.legit: Correct. A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering.

questions.prompt: What do red-black tree invariants provide?

answers.text: They force the tree to be perfectly balanced.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance.
feedback.sus: Correct. Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance.

answers.text: Every root-to-leaf path has exactly the same total number of nodes.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path.
feedback.sus: Correct. Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path.

answers.text: Red-black tree lookup takes O(1) time in the worst case.

feedback.legit: Not quite. The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case.
feedback.sus: Correct. The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures
